_comment: D333 PLAYABLE FRANKLIN, November 30, 1864. Single-phase Franklin/Nashville lane battle built from the D332 spec. Confederate infantry attacks the Federal main line around Carter House / Carter cotton gin / Columbia Pike. Active assaulting strength is kept near 18,000-20,000, not the broader Army of Tennessee present figure. Exact brigade/division game strengths are abstractions marked Inferred where not source-pinned. No per-battle damage/firepower/casualty/winner fudge.

sources: Battle of Franklin Trust, history page
National Park Service, Battle of Franklin Special Resource Study
War of the Rebellion / Official Records, Series I, Vol. XLV, Pt. 1
Civil War Trust / Wikipedia-hosted Franklin OOB pages
HistoryNet, "The Myth of the 5 Dead Rebel Generals"

weather.note: The assault is modeled as a late-afternoon daylight attack over open ground into visible works. Fog is off by default because Franklin's teaching point is open approach, breastworks, abatis, reserve counterattack, and command choice.
sources: Battle of Franklin Trust, history page
NPS Battle of Franklin Special Resource Study
Official Records, Series I, Vol. XLV, Pt. 1

franklin.name: Franklin - The Carter House Line
franklin.place: Franklin, Tennessee - Columbia Pike, Carter House, and Carter cotton gin
franklin.blurb: After the Spring Hill failure, Hood sends the Army of Tennessee into a daylight frontal assault against Schofield's prepared Franklin line. Wagner's exposed brigades fall back through the works, Brown and Cleburne drive into the Carter House sector, and Opdycke's reserve counterattack seals the breach.
franklin.provenance: Verified (high) for date, commanders, broad strengths, Carter House / cotton gin / Columbia Pike terrain, Wagner forward-line trap, Opdycke counterattack, casualty direction, and Franklin-Nashville operational frame. The active Confederate assaulting force is modeled near 18,000-20,000, not the broader army-present figure. Inferred (medium) for per-game-unit strengths and some battery groupings. Anti-Lost-Cause frame: Franklin was a disastrous command decision after Spring Hill, not noble martyrdom.
franklin._assaultDoctrineNote: The AI Confederate attacker models a dense frontal assault into prepared works. This is an input/doctrine choice reflecting historical formation and tactical problem, not an output fudge.

menu.title: FRANKLIN (1864)
menu.deck: Hood's Army of Tennessee attacks Schofield's Carter House line - break the pike gap, or hold the works.
menu.aria: Franklin, November 1864 - the Carter House line. Command the Confederate assault or the Union defense.

sides.heading: Franklin - Take Which Command?
sides.intro: November 30, 1864 - a late-day assault after Spring Hill.

CS.title: Command Hood's assault
CS.deck: Drive Cheatham's and Stewart's men across the open ground, exploit the Columbia Pike gap, and break the Carter House line.

US.title: Hold for Schofield
US.deck: Use the works, Fort Granger's guns, and Opdycke's reserve to seal the breach and keep the Harpeth crossing open.

sides.foot: The other army is commanded by the AI. Franklin is a single-phase battle; Nashville remains a separate later battle.

brief.attack: Spring Hill failed to trap Schofield. Now Hood orders the attack at Franklin. Your assault must cross open ground, push through Wagner's broken line, and seize the Carter House / Columbia Pike works. This is your war: history says the frontal assault ruined the Army of Tennessee; coordinate well enough to do better.
brief.defend: Cox's line bends around the Carter House and cotton gin. Wagner's forward line is exposed, but Opdycke's brigade waits in reserve. This is your war: hold the pike gap and keep the army's withdrawal over the Harpeth intact.

objective.name: Carter House and Columbia Pike main line
objective.note: The Federal main line at the Carter House / Carter cotton gin sector. Holding this radius represents keeping the Columbia Pike works and breach area in Union hands. Historically the Confederate assault pierced but did not hold the line.

leaders._note: Ranks are battle-date ranks. Hood is encoded with temporary full-general grade and permanent lieutenant-general nuance; Schofield and Stanley are major generals; Cox and Wagner are brigadier generals; Opdycke is a colonel; Cheatham, Cleburne, and Brown are major generals. The six general-officer death nuance: John C. Carter was mortally wounded at Franklin and died December 10, not killed outright on the field.

US.name: Maj. Gen. John M. Schofield
US.note: Major General commanding the Union field force at Franklin. (Verified)

US.name: Brig. Gen. Jacob D. Cox
US.note: Brigadier General directing the main Franklin defensive line from the Carter House. (Verified)

US.name: Maj. Gen. David S. Stanley
US.note: Major General commanding IV Corps at Franklin; wounded in the battle. (Verified)

US.name: Brig. Gen. George D. Wagner
US.note: Brigadier General whose exposed forward line broke back through the main works. (Verified)

US.name: Col. Emerson Opdycke
US.note: Colonel commanding the reserve brigade that counterattacked near the Carter House. Do not promote him at Franklin. (Verified)

CS.name: Gen. John Bell Hood (temporary grade)
CS.note: Temporary full General commanding the Army of Tennessee; permanent grade lieutenant general. (Verified)

CS.name: Maj. Gen. Benjamin F. Cheatham
CS.note: Major General commanding the corps that included Cleburne, Brown, and Bate divisions. (Verified)

CS.name: Maj. Gen. Patrick R. Cleburne
CS.note: Major General killed at Franklin. Do not make him lieutenant general. (Verified)

CS.name: Maj. Gen. John C. Brown
CS.note: Major General whose division entered the center around the Carter House before the counterattack. (Verified)

CS.name: Lt. Gen. Alexander P. Stewart
CS.note: Lieutenant General commanding one of Hood's corps at Franklin. (Verified)

CS.name: Cowan's Mississippi Battery
CS.note: Representative Confederate artillery support; most Franklin damage came from infantry assault into works, not a gun duel. Universal gun model: 4 guns. (Verified artillery identity; Inferred placement)

reinforcements.name: Opdycke's Reserve Brigade
reinforcements.entry: from reserve behind the Carter House
reinforcements.note: Reserve brigade that refused Wagner's forward line and counterattacked to seal the breach. Opdycke was a colonel. (Verified identity/rank; Inferred strength/timing)

reinforcements.name: Stanley's Reserve Elements
reinforcements.entry: from IV Corps support behind the main line
reinforcements.note: Reserve abstraction for Stanley's command and the counterattack around the line. (Verified commander; Inferred strength/timing)

reinforcements.name: Cheatham's Corps Support
reinforcements.note: Support abstraction keeps active Confederate assault strength near the D332 18,000-20,000 range. (Verified sector; Inferred strength/timing)

US.US: History holds. The Carter House / Columbia Pike line still stands. Wagner's forward line made the danger worse, but Opdycke's reserve and the main works sealed the breach.
US.CS: History is overturned against your army. Hood's assault has broken and held the Carter House line, the result the Army of Tennessee could not achieve in 1864.

CS.US: History is overturned. Your attack has carried and held the Carter House line that Hood's men historically could not keep.
CS.CS: History holds. The assault pierced the line but could not hold it; the Army of Tennessee paid for the frontal attack and moved toward destruction at Nashville.

endNote.draw: A torn line and a shattered field: Franklin remains unresolved in this run, but the cost of sending infantry into prepared works is plain.

teaching.title: Franklin - A Frontal Assault Into Works

cards.head: Hood's charge, not noble tragedy
cards.body: Franklin followed the missed opportunity at Spring Hill. Hood chose a daylight frontal assault across open ground against prepared works. The game treats that as a command decision and tactical problem, not romance.
cards.provenance: Verified - American Battlefield Trust; Battle of Franklin Trust; NPS special-resource study.

cards.head: A breach is not a victory
cards.body: The Confederate attack entered the Carter House sector near the Columbia Pike, but Opdycke's reserve and nearby Federal troops counterattacked. Franklin teaches why reserve depth and line repair matter.
cards.provenance: Verified - ABT and Battle of Franklin Trust for the Carter House fighting and Opdycke counterattack.

cards.head: Use the assaulting force, not the whole army
cards.body: Hood had a larger army present, but the active assault is closer to 18,000-20,000 men. The scenario uses that game-scale force and labels exact unit strengths as inferred when the source packet does not pin them. John C. Carter's place in the general-officer loss list is also handled carefully: he was mortally wounded on November 30 and died December 10.
cards.provenance: Verified for broad totals and roughly 20,000 assaulting Confederates; inferred for game-unit splits.

cards.head: The field did not end at the works
cards.body: Carter House, Carnton, and the Confederate cemetery carry the battle's human aftermath. The lesson is command responsibility, civilian proximity, and the ruined Army of Tennessee, not a glory narrative.
cards.provenance: Verified - NPS special-resource study and Battle of Franklin Trust interpretation.

codex.title: Franklin - November 30, 1864
codex.summary: Hood's Army of Tennessee assaulted Schofield's prepared Franklin line around the Carter House and Columbia Pike. The attack briefly broke into the center but was repulsed with disastrous Confederate losses, setting up the army's collapse at Nashville.
